steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Find the third angle in triangle QXS
steps.content:
In triangle QXS, we have two angles: \angle SQX = 48 degrees and \angle SXQ = 86 degrees. Using the angle sum property:
$$\angle QSX = 180^{\circ} - 48^{\circ} - 86^{\circ} = 46^{\circ}$$
This is the angle at vertex S between ray SQ (along line PV) and ray SX (toward point X). Since W lies on segment SX, this same angle applies to ray SW.

steps.title: Use vertical angles at W to find ∠RWS
steps.content:
At point W, lines RU and SX intersect. We're given \angle SWU = 85 degrees.

Since R, W, and U are collinear on line RU, rays WR and WU point in opposite directions. Therefore:
$$\angle RWS + \angle SWU = 180^{\circ}$$
$$\angle RWS = 180^{\circ} - 85^{\circ} = 95^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Find ∠TRU using triangle RWS
steps.content:
In triangle $RWS$, we now have:
  • $\angle RWS=95^\circ$
  • $\angle WSR=46^\circ$, the same as $\angle QSX$
Use the triangle angle sum: $$\angle WRS=180^\circ-95^\circ-46^\circ=39^\circ.$$ Since $W$ lies on line $RU$, ray $RW$ points in the same direction as ray $RU$. Since $S$ and $T$ lie on line $PV$, ray $RS$ points in the same direction as ray $RT$. Therefore, $\angle TRU=39^\circ$.
